text:
We report the topotactic oxidative insertion of weakly coordinating anions into the redox-active metal–organic framework (MOF) Fe₂(dobpdc). While insertion reactions involving cations are well-documented in battery chemistry, the reversible accommodation of large anions like BF₄⁻ and PF₆⁻ is typically hindered by the rigidity of 3D host lattices. Here, we demonstrate that Fe₂(dobpdc) utilizes its expansive one-dimensional channels and dense chains of iron(II) centers to overcome these limitations. Chemical and electrochemical oxidation transform the material into a mixed-valence state, evidenced by the appearance of a broad intervalence charge-transfer band in the near-infrared region and a modest contraction of the unit cell. 

Characterization via ⁵⁷Fe Mössbauer spectroscopy and X-ray diffraction confirms that the framework maintains structural integrity through stoichiometric metal-based redox processes. Additionally, studies on the isostructural Mg-based analogue reveal that ligand-centered oxidation is also possible, though less reversible than the metal-based transition. The electrochemical performance of the MOF shows significant dependency on anion size and solvent environment, reflecting the unique dynamics of ion transport within nanoconfined pores. Leveraging these properties, we developed a prototype sodium dual-ion battery using Fe₂(dobpdc) as the cathode. The system exhibits a high initial reduction potential and achieves a maximum specific energy of 316 Wh/kg, maintaining stability over 50 cycles. This work highlights the potential of redox-active MOFs as high-energy density electrodes for advanced dual-ion storage systems.
